questions.stem: What does the command `grep "error" log.txt` do?
options: Counts how many lines are in `log.txt`
Prints the lines of `log.txt` that contain the text `error`
questions.explanation: `grep` searches a file for a pattern and prints the lines that match. So `grep "error" log.txt` outputs every line of `log.txt` that contains the string `error`. It does not modify the file.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What does the option in `grep -c "fail" results.txt` make `grep` do?
options: Print the matching lines in colour
Print only the count of matching lines
Count the characters in the file
questions.explanation: The `-c` option makes `grep` report a count: the number of lines that match, rather than the lines themselves. So `grep -c "fail" results.txt` prints a single number, how many lines contain `fail`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What does `wc -l names.txt` report?
questions.explanation: `wc` is the word-count tool, and the `-l` option restricts it to counting lines. So `wc -l names.txt` prints how many lines the file has. The options `-w` and `-c` count words and characters instead.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: The vertical bar in `cat data.txt | sort` is called a pipe. What does it do here?
options: Saves the output of `cat` into a file called `sort`
Sends the output of `cat data.txt` as the input to `sort`
Runs `cat` and `sort` at completely separate times
questions.explanation: A pipe `|` connects two commands so that the output of the one on the left becomes the input of the one on the right. Here the lines produced by `cat data.txt` are fed straight into `sort`, which then sorts them.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What does the redirection in `ls > files.txt` do?
options: Writes the output of `ls` into the file `files.txt`, overwriting it
questions.explanation: The `>` operator redirects a command's standard output into a file, creating it or replacing its contents. So `ls > files.txt` saves the directory listing into `files.txt`, discarding whatever was there before.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: How does `>>` differ from `>` in `echo hi >> log.txt` ?
options: `>>` appends to the file instead of overwriting it
questions.explanation: A single `>` overwrites the target file, but `>>` appends, adding the new output to the end while keeping the existing contents. So `echo hi >> log.txt` adds a line `hi` without erasing the file.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Given a file `marks.txt` of numbers, what does `sort marks.txt` do by default?
options: Sorts the lines in reverse order
Sorts the lines in ascending order, comparing them as text
questions.explanation: By default `sort` orders lines ascending using a text (lexicographic) comparison, so `10` would come before `9`. To compare them as numbers you must add the `-n` option, as in `sort -n marks.txt`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Why is `-n` needed in `sort -n marks.txt` when the file holds numbers?
options: It compares the lines as numbers rather than as text
questions.explanation: The `-n` option tells `sort` to interpret the lines as numeric values, so `9` correctly comes before `10`. Without it, a plain text sort would place `10` before `9` because it compares character by character.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What is required for `uniq` to remove duplicate lines correctly, as in `sort data.txt | uniq` ?
options: Nothing; `uniq` finds duplicates anywhere in the file
The duplicate lines must already be adjacent, which is why the input is sorted first
questions.explanation: `uniq` only collapses duplicate lines that are next to each other, so the usual pattern is to `sort` the data first to bring identical lines together, then pipe into `uniq`. Duplicates that are not adjacent would otherwise be missed.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What does the option in `uniq -c` add to each output line?
options: A line number from the original file
The character count of the line
A count of how many times the line occurred
questions.explanation: `uniq -c` prefixes each distinct line with the number of times it appeared consecutively. Combined with a prior `sort`, this is the classic way to tally how often each value occurs.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: In a comma-separated file, what does `cut -d',' -f2 data.csv` extract?
options: The first comma-separated field of each line
The second character of each line
The second comma-separated field of each line
questions.explanation: `-d','` sets the field delimiter to a comma, and `-f2` selects field number two. So `cut -d',' -f2 data.csv` prints the second column of each line. By default `cut` would split on tabs instead.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What does `awk '{print $1}' data.txt` print?
options: The first whitespace-separated field of each line
The first character of each line
The number of fields on each line
questions.explanation: In `awk`, `$1` is the first field of the current line (fields are separated by whitespace by default). The action `{print $1}` runs on every line, so this command prints the first column of `data.txt`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: In `awk`, what does the special variable `$0` refer to?
options: The number of the current line
questions.explanation: `$0` stands for the whole current record, that is the entire line. Individual fields are `$1`, `$2` and so on, while `NF` holds the number of fields and `NR` the current record number.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What does the stream editor command `sed 's/cat/dog/' pets.txt` do?
options: Replaces the first `cat` on each line with `dog` in the output
questions.explanation: The `sed` substitute command `s/old/new/` replaces the first match of `old` with `new` on each line. So `sed 's/cat/dog/' pets.txt` prints the file with the first `cat` on every line changed to `dog`. Adding a trailing `g` would replace all of them.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What extra effect does the trailing `g` have in `sed 's/a/A/g' file.txt` ?
options: It replaces only the first `a` on each line
It replaces every `a` on each line, not just the first
questions.explanation: Without a flag, `sed` replaces only the first match on each line. The `g` (global) flag tells it to replace every occurrence on the line. So `s/a/A/g` turns all lowercase `a` characters into `A`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which command makes `grep` ignore the difference between uppercase and lowercase letters?
questions.explanation: The `-i` option makes the match case-insensitive, so `grep -i error log.txt` finds `error`, `Error` and `ERROR` alike. `-c` counts, `-n` adds line numbers, and `-v` inverts the match.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What does `grep -v "ok" status.txt` print?
options: The file with `ok` removed from each line
A count of the lines containing `ok`
The lines that do not contain `ok`
questions.explanation: The `-v` option inverts the match, so `grep -v "ok" status.txt` prints exactly the lines that do not contain `ok`. It is useful for filtering out unwanted lines.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Suppose `data.txt` contains the three lines `banana`, `apple`, `cherry`. What does `sort data.txt | head -n 1` print?
questions.explanation: `sort` puts the lines in ascending alphabetical order, giving `apple`, `banana`, `cherry`. Piping into `head -n 1` keeps only the first line of that sorted output, which is `apple`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What does the redirection in `wc -l < data.txt` do with the `<` operator?
options: Sends the contents of `data.txt` into `wc -l` as its input
questions.explanation: The `<` operator redirects input, feeding the contents of `data.txt` into the command's standard input. So `wc -l < data.txt` counts the lines coming from that file. (`>` and `>>` redirect output instead.)
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which command joins two files line by line, side by side into columns?
questions.explanation: `paste` merges files horizontally, placing corresponding lines from each file next to each other separated by a tab. `cat` joins them end to end (vertically), while `cut` extracts columns from a single file.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Which tool is designed to extract selected fields or columns from each line of a file?
questions.explanation: `cut` extracts portions of each line, either by character position or, with `-d` and `-f`, by delimited fields. `wc` counts, `man` shows manuals, and `touch` creates or updates files.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What does `awk -F',' '{print $2}' data.csv` print?
options: The first comma-separated field of each line
The second comma-separated field of each line
The second character of each line
The number of commas on each line
questions.explanation: The `-F','` option sets the field separator to a comma, so fields are split on commas. The action `{print $2}` then prints the second such field of every line, that is the second column of `data.csv`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: A common pipeline to list the unique values and how often each appears is `sort file | uniq -c`. What part does the `sort` play?
options: It groups identical lines together so that `uniq` can count them
It reverses the order of the lines
questions.explanation: Because `uniq` only merges adjacent duplicates, the `sort` first brings all identical lines next to each other. Then `uniq -c` collapses each run and prefixes it with a count, producing a frequency tally.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: What does `wc data.txt` print when run with no options?
options: Three numbers: the line count, word count and character count
questions.explanation: With no option, `wc` prints three counts for the file in order: the number of lines, the number of words and the number of characters (bytes), followed by the file name. The `-l`, `-w` and `-c` options select just one of these.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Choose the right tool for each text-processing task.
questions.solution: To print only the lines of `log.txt` that contain `error`, use {{1}}. To print just the first whitespace field of each line, use {{2}}. To count how many lines are in the file, use {{3}}.
questions.explanation: `grep error` prints matching lines, `awk '{print $1}'` prints the first field of each line, and `wc -l` counts lines.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Pick the correct redirection or pipe operator for each goal.
questions.solution: To save the output of `ls` into a new file, overwriting it, use {{1}}. To add output to the end of an existing file, use {{2}}. To feed one command's output into another, use {{3}}.
questions.explanation: `>` overwrites a file with the output, `>>` appends to it, and `|` pipes one command's output into the next command's input.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: The file `scores.txt` contains exactly these five lines: `pass`, `fail`, `pass`, `pass`, `fail`. Predict the counts.
questions.solution: The number of lines printed by `grep pass scores.txt` is {{1}}. The single number printed by `grep -c fail scores.txt` is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: Three of the five lines are `pass`, so `grep pass` prints 3 lines. Two lines are `fail`, and `grep -c fail` reports that count as the number 2.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Choose the option that gives each `grep` or `sort` behaviour.
questions.solution: To make `grep` ignore upper and lower case, add the option {{1}}. To make `sort` compare lines as numbers rather than text, add the option {{2}}.
questions.explanation: `grep -i` searches case-insensitively, and `sort -n` performs a numeric sort so that `9` comes before `10`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Select the `awk` field reference for each description.
questions.solution: The reference that prints the second whitespace-separated field of each line is {{1}}. The reference that prints the entire current line is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: In `awk`, `$2` is the second field while `$0` is the whole line, so `{print $2}` prints the second column and `{print $0}` echoes each line in full.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)

questions.stem: Reason about a frequency-counting pipeline.
questions.solution: To list each distinct value together with how many times it occurs, the pipeline is `sort file | uniq -c`; the option that adds the count is {{1}}. The step that must come first so that identical lines are adjacent is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: `uniq -c` prefixes each group with its count, and because `uniq` only merges adjacent duplicates, the lines must be `sort`ed first to bring identical values together.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Introduction to Scientific Computing course (Linux and Python)
